description: Observed a natural soft slab on a northeast-facing slope near 9,800 ft in upper Days Fork. Approximately 150 feet wide and 1 to 2 feet deep. Crown was clearly visible from the skin track. The slide failed on a persistent weak layer of faceted snow roughly 2 feet below the surface. Debris ran about 600 vertical feet into the drainage bottom. No one was caught.
weather_notes: Partly cloudy, light SW wind, temp ~28°F at ridgeline

description: Touring along the Park City ridgeline we spotted a recent hard slab avalanche on the north face of Scotts Bowl. Estimated 250 feet wide with a 3-foot crown. It appeared to have run naturally sometime overnight. The bed surface was smooth and pencil-hard, consistent with a wind slab failing on an old surface hoar layer. Large debris blocks in the runout zone.
weather_notes: Clear skies, moderate W wind with gusts to 30 mph on the ridge, temp ~22°F

description: Ski patrol triggered a soft slab with explosives on the east face of Cardiac Bowl at approximately 10,500 ft. The slide was about 200 feet wide and 18 inches deep. It ran on a layer of near-surface facets that formed during the clear weather window last week. Several shooting cracks were observed in adjacent terrain before the shot.
weather_notes: Overcast, light snowfall beginning, calm wind, temp ~24°F

description: Observed a wet loose avalanche on a southeast-facing slope in upper Silver Fork around 1 PM as solar warming intensified. The slide started as a point release near a rock band and entrained wet surface snow as it ran approximately 400 vertical feet. Estimated 80 feet wide at the widest point. Surface was becoming isothermal and saturated in the afternoon sun.
weather_notes: Mostly sunny, calm wind, temp ~36°F in the sun at 9,400 ft

description: A skier triggered a wind slab on a northwest-facing slope at the top of Cardiff Fork near 10,000 ft. The slide was approximately 180 feet wide and 2.5 feet deep. The skier was carried about 100 feet but was not buried. The slab appeared to be comprised of recent wind-deposited snow sitting on a faceted crust interface. Cross-loaded features in the area showed obvious signs of wind effect.
weather_notes: Broken clouds, strong W wind 25-40 mph on ridge, temp ~18°F

description: Dug a snowpit on a north-facing slope at 9,600 ft in Grizzly Gulch. Total snow depth was 135 inches. Found a layer of 1-2 mm facets sitting on a melt-freeze crust roughly 90 cm below the surface. An extended column test produced propagation at this layer with moderate force (ECTP 18). Above the weak layer, the snowpack was well-bonded with 4F to 1F hardness. This persistent weak layer continues to be reactive in stability tests on sheltered north aspects.
weather_notes: Clear, calm, temp ~20°F at pit location

location_name: Mill B South Fork, Big Cottonwood Canyon
description: Quick hand pit on a west-facing slope below treeline in Mill B South Fork. 105 inches total depth. Upper 18 inches of new snow from the recent storm sitting on a 1F pencil-hard old surface. Tap test failed easily at the new/old interface. Below that, the mid-pack is well-settled and round-grained. Did not find the deeper persistent layer at this elevation. Surface skiing conditions were excellent — dry blower powder in sheltered terrain.
weather_notes: Overcast, light snowfall, calm wind, temp ~26°F

description: Full snowpit at 9,200 ft on a northeast-facing slope in Mineral Fork. Total depth 118 inches. The primary concern is a 3 cm thick layer of depth hoar sitting on a rain crust about 110 cm down. Extended column test failed with full propagation on moderate taps (ECTP 14). Compression test also failed at that layer (CT 13 Q2). On the approach, experienced one notable collapse (whumpf) while crossing a bench at roughly the same elevation. The upper snowpack above this layer is right-side-up and progressively hardening.
weather_notes: Mostly cloudy, light NW wind, temp ~25°F

description: Dug a snowpit near the summit of Ben Lomond on a northeast aspect at 9,400 ft. Total depth 78 inches — notably thinner snowpack than the central Wasatch. Found a layer of faceted snow near the ground approximately 70 cm down. Stability tests were unreactive (ECTX, CT 30). The facets are well-developed but the slab above is thin and soft, which may not be providing enough stress to initiate a fracture. Skied a conservative line and the snow quality was pleasant — settled powder with a supportable surface.
weather_notes: Partly cloudy, light W wind, temp ~23°F

description: Observed shooting cracks up to 20 feet long while approaching Cascade Cirque on a north-facing slope near 10,400 ft. Dug a quick pit and found a reactive layer of surface hoar buried about 60 cm below the surface. An extended column test produced sudden planar fracture with propagation on the 10th tap (ECTP 10). The surface hoar layer was 5-8 mm and sitting beneath a slab of storm and wind-deposited snow. This result is concerning and we decided not to ski any steep terrain. Backed off and chose a lower-angle alternative.
weather_notes: Clear, moderate SW wind, temp ~20°F at ridgeline

location_name: Alta Collins Trailhead, Little Cottonwood Canyon
description: Early morning observation from the Alta Collins trailhead. Clear skies with good visibility. Temperature was 15°F at 8,600 ft at 7 AM. Winds were calm in the canyon bottom. No new snow overnight. The road was in good condition with some compact snow/ice. Excellent visibility up-canyon — could see the ridgeline clearly.
weather_notes: Clear, calm, temp 15°F, no precipitation, visibility unlimited

location_name: Brighton Ski Resort, Big Cottonwood Canyon
description: Heavy snowfall at Brighton with 14 inches of new snow in the past 24 hours as of noon. Snow was falling at S2 intensity (moderate) with no signs of letting up. Temperature at 10,000 ft was 21°F. Winds were strong out of the west at 25-35 mph with gusts higher on the ridge. Significant transport and loading of east-facing slopes was visible. Visibility was reduced to 200-300 feet during heavier bands.
weather_notes: Overcast, heavy snowfall (S2), strong W wind 25-35 mph, temp 21°F, visibility 200-300 ft

location_name: Snowbird Ski Resort, Little Cottonwood Canyon
description: Strong westerly winds at Hidden Peak with sustained speeds of 35-45 mph and gusts exceeding 60 mph. Significant blowing and drifting snow. Visibility on the upper mountain was severely reduced. Temperature was 14°F at 11,000 ft. Large plumes of snow were visible blowing off the ridgeline. Cross-loaded gullies on the north and northwest faces were receiving heavy deposits of wind-transported snow. Travel on the upper mountain was difficult.
weather_notes: Overcast, blowing snow, extreme W wind 35-45 mph gusting 60+, temp 14°F, visibility poor

description: Afternoon temperatures at 7,400 ft in Porter Fork reached 42°F by 5 PM — significantly warmer than the morning. South and southwest-facing slopes below treeline had a noticeable wet surface with the top 2-3 inches becoming moist. Small rollerballs were observed on steep south-facing terrain nearby. The warming trend was rapid, with temperatures rising roughly 15 degrees between 10 AM and 3 PM.
weather_notes: Mostly sunny, light S wind, temp 42°F, warming trend

location_name: Solitude Ski Resort, Big Cottonwood Canyon
description: Midday weather check at Solitude near the Roundhouse area at 9,400 ft. Scattered clouds with good visibility. Temperature was 28°F. Winds were light out of the southwest at 5-10 mph. No precipitation. Snow surface was firm with a thin sun crust forming on east-facing slopes exposed to the morning sun. North-facing terrain still had soft, dry snow from the previous week's storm. Pleasant touring conditions overall.
weather_notes: Scattered clouds, light SW wind 5-10 mph, temp 28°F, no precipitation

description: Experienced multiple collapses (whumpfs) and shooting cracks while touring through White Pine basin on northeast-facing slopes between 9,000 and 9,500 ft. The most alarming collapse occurred at 9,200 ft where a loud whumpf was felt and a shooting crack propagated roughly 50 feet in front of us. We immediately retreated to lower-angle terrain. These are clear signs of a reactive persistent weak layer that is not yet healing on shaded aspects at this elevation band.
weather_notes: Partly cloudy, calm wind, temp ~26°F

description: Some minor wind loading and associated localized cracking along the BCC/Millcreek ridge above 10,000 ft. No collapsing was heard or felt. The wind slabs were mostly small and isolated, found on northwest and north-facing features immediately below the ridgeline. One shooting crack extended about 15 feet on a wind-loaded rollover near the top of the ridge. These wind slabs are touchy but appeared to be relatively thin (6-12 inches). Avoided committing to any steep wind-loaded features.
weather_notes: Overcast, moderate W wind 15-25 mph on the ridge, temp ~22°F

description: Observed several recent natural avalanches on north-facing slopes near Mt. Ogden while touring in the Snowbasin backcountry. At least three separate slides were visible, ranging from D1 to D2 in size. They appeared to have run within the last 24-48 hours based on the freshness of the debris. While traversing a flat bench at 9,400 ft, experienced a significant collapse that dropped the snow surface by about an inch. The collapsing suggests the persistent weak layer is widespread and still active in the Ogden mountains.
weather_notes: Few clouds, light NW wind, temp ~24°F

description: Observed numerous rollerballs and small pinwheels on south-facing slopes in Butler Fork below treeline during a warm afternoon. Temperatures had risen steadily through the morning and the snow surface was becoming noticeably wet and heavy below about 8,500 ft on solar aspects. A few small loose wet slides (D1) had also released naturally on steep south-facing terrain above us. The rapid warming and wet activity prompted us to finish our tour early and exit before conditions deteriorated further.
weather_notes: Mostly sunny, light S wind, temp ~39°F at 8,400 ft

description: Toured into Dry Creek drainage on the west side of Timpanogos. Measured 16 inches of new snow at the trailhead and 20+ inches at 9,000 ft on northwest aspects. Strong westerly winds had created large drifts and cornices along the ridgeline. Observed at least two recent natural soft slab avalanches on wind-loaded northwest-facing slopes, roughly D1.5 to D2 in size. The combination of significant new snow, strong wind loading, and natural avalanche activity made conditions feel very dangerous. We turned around at 9,000 ft and did not proceed to steeper terrain.
weather_notes: Overcast, moderate to strong W wind, temp ~19°F, light snowfall continuing
